Today is February 21, 2026. US President Donald Trump recently announced a new global tariff of 15% following a Supreme Court ruling. On what specific date are these new tariffs scheduled to come into force?
(A) Tuesday 24 February
(B) Friday 20 February
(C) Saturday 21 February
(D) Monday 23 February
(E) Wednesday 25 February
explanation: According to the article, the new 15% tariffs "will come into force on Tuesday 24 February."
model_output:
On February 20, 2026, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that President Donald Trump's previous tariffs were imposed unlawfully. In response, on February 21, 2026, President Trump announced an immediate increase of the global import tariff from 10% to 15%, effective immediately. ([theguardian.com](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/feb/21/trump-tariffs-15-percent?utm_source=openai))

Therefore, the new 15% tariffs are scheduled to come into force on Monday, February 23, 2026.

<final_answer>
(D)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. President Trump has criticized the Supreme Court justices who voted to strike down his global tariffs. Which justice wrote the majority opinion for the 6-3 decision?
(A) Neil Gorsuch
(B) John Roberts
(C) Amy Coney Barrett
(D) Clarence Thomas
(E) Brett Kavanaugh
explanation: The article states that "Chief Justice John Roberts is a George W Bush pick who wrote the majority opinion."
model_output:
In the Supreme Court's 6-3 decision to strike down former President Donald Trump's global tariffs, Chief Justice John Roberts authored the majority opinion. The Court held that the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) does not grant the president the authority to unilaterally impose such tariffs. ([washingtonpost.com](https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2026/02/20/trump-tariffs-supreme-court-decision/?utm_source=openai))

Justices Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett, both appointed by President Trump, joined the majority opinion. Justices Clarence Thomas, Brett Kavanaugh, and Samuel Alito dissented. ([washingtonpost.com](https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2026/02/20/trump-tariffs-supreme-court-decision/?utm_source=openai))

Therefore, the correct answer is:

<final_answer>
(B)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. OpenAI recently revealed that it had banned a ChatGPT account owned by Jesse Van Rootselaar, the suspect in the Tumbler Ridge shooting. In what month and year did OpenAI first identify and ban this account?
(A) February 2026
(B) September 2025
(C) June 2025
(D) January 2025
(E) July 2025
explanation: The article states that OpenAI "had identified an account owned by Jesse Van Rootselaar in June 2025 under abuse and enforcement detection."
model_output:
OpenAI identified and banned the ChatGPT account of Jesse Van Rootselaar in June 2025 for violating its usage policy. The company detected suspicious activity through its abuse detection systems and considered alerting Canadian authorities. However, OpenAI determined that the activity did not meet the threshold for referral to law enforcement, as it lacked imminent and credible threats of physical harm. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/d574e2703a6e9472b59aa3a5371c57a5?utm_source=openai))

Therefore, the correct answer is (C) June 2025.

<final_answer>
(C)
</final_answer>

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. NASA administrator Jared Isaacman announced on Saturday that the highly anticipated Artemis II lunar mission will almost definitely be delayed. What specific technical issue was cited as the cause for this expected delay?
(A) An interruption in the flow of helium required for launch operations.
(B) Hydrogen leaks detected in the rocket's seals and filters.
(C) A failure to fuel the rocket with 730,000 gallons of propellant over 50 hours.
(D) An issue with the space capsule's life support systems.
(E) A delay in the delivery of the SpaceX Starship lander.
explanation: The article reports: "But overnight on Friday, engineers observed an interruption in the flow of helium required for launch operations. 'This will almost assuredly impact the March launch window,' NASA said in a statement..."
model_output:
NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman announced that the Artemis II lunar mission is expected to be delayed due to a technical issue with the rocket's helium system. Specifically, an interruption in the flow of helium required for engine purging and fuel tank pressurization was identified during routine operations. This problem necessitates returning the Space Launch System (SLS) rocket to the Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center for repairs, which will likely impact the March launch window. ([nasa.gov](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/02/21/nasa-troubleshooting-artemis-ii-rocket-upper-stage-issue-preparing-to-roll-back/?utm_source=openai))

The helium system is crucial for maintaining proper pressure and conditions in the rocket's propellant tanks and for purging the engines. The malfunction could be due to a faulty filter, valve, or connection plate. Engineers are actively investigating the issue to determine the exact cause and to implement necessary repairs. ([nasa.gov](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/02/21/nasa-troubleshooting-artemis-ii-rocket-upper-stage-issue-preparing-to-roll-back/?utm_source=openai))

This issue is separate from previous hydrogen fuel leaks that had also caused delays in the mission's timeline. NASA is working diligently to address these technical challenges to ensure the safety and success of the Artemis II mission. ([nasa.gov](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/02/21/nasa-troubleshooting-artemis-ii-rocket-upper-stage-issue-preparing-to-roll-back/?utm_source=openai))

<final_answer>
(A)
</final_answer>

explanation: The article quotes Khamenei's post directly: "The US President constantly says that the US has sent a warship toward Iran. Of course, a warship is a dangerous piece of military hardware."

He further stated, "However, more dangerous than that warship is the weapon that can send that warship to the bottom of the sea." ([indiatoday.in](https://www.indiatoday.in/amp/world/story/iran-supreme-leader-warns-us-warships-can-be-sunk-amid-nuclear-talks-glbs-2870509-2026-02-18?utm_source=openai))

<final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. As the US, Canada, and Mexico prepare for the upcoming USMCA trade deal review, US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer highlighted specific obstacles in negotiations with Canada. What specific action by Canada did Greer cite as a "big challenge" during an interview?
(A) Canada's refusal to lift the 35% tariff on American fentanyl and drug imports.
(B) The broadcasting of an anti-tariffs advert sponsored by the province of Ontario during the World Series.
(C) Canada's refusal to sell US wine and spirits on their shelves.
(D) The failure to integrate the North American economies under the 1990s free trade deal.
(E) The decision to prioritize trade ties with Mexico over the United States before the July 1 deadline.
explanation: The article quotes Jamieson Greer stating: "They continue to have certain barriers. They refuse to sell US wine and spirits on their shelves.,' he said. 'There are a variety of issues... and this makes it a big challenge and an obstacle for starting real negotiations with them.'"
model_output:
In a recent interview, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer highlighted that Canada's refusal to sell U.S. wine and spirits on their shelves is a significant obstacle in the USMCA trade negotiations. He stated that Canada continues to have certain barriers and is not addressing them, making it challenging to start real negotiations. ([eenews.net](https://www.eenews.net/articles/us-trade-rep-says-canada-making-it-hard-to-begin-talks-on-usmca-review/?utm_source=openai))

This issue pertains to Canada's longstanding market access barriers imposed by provincial liquor control boards, which hinder exports of U.S. wine, beer, and spirits to Canada. Additionally, the province of Ontario has ordered its state-run liquor stores to remove American alcoholic products from their shelves until the U.S. lifts tariffs imposed on Canada. ([eenews.net](https://www.eenews.net/articles/us-trade-rep-says-canada-making-it-hard-to-begin-talks-on-usmca-review/?utm_source=openai))

Therefore, the specific action by Canada that Greer cited as a "big challenge" is:

<final_answer>
(C)
</final_answer>

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. NASA is aiming for an early March launch for its Artemis II mission after completing a successful "wet dress rehearsal". However, a previous rehearsal attempt in early February had to be aborted. What specifically caused the early termination of that first rehearsal?
(A) The crew was required to go into mandatory quarantine.
(B) An interruption in the flow of helium used to pressurize fuel tanks.
(C) Unfavorable weather conditions at the Kennedy Space Center.
(D) A hydrogen fuel leak at the launch pad.
(E) Issues with the Orion capsule's life support systems.
explanation: The text notes: "The first rehearsal, which took place at the beginning of February, ended early because of a hydrogen fuel leak at the launch pad."
model_output:
During the first Artemis II wet dress rehearsal on February 2, 2026, NASA encountered a liquid hydrogen leak at the interface of the tail service mast umbilical. This issue led to the termination of the countdown at the T-5:15 minute mark. ([nasa.gov](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/02/03/artemis-ii-wet-dress-rehearsal-test-terminated-at-t-515/?utm_source=openai))

The tail service mast umbilical is a critical component that connects the rocket to the ground support equipment, supplying essential propellants and electrical power. A leak in this area can compromise the fueling process and overall safety of the mission.

In response to this problem, NASA engineers replaced two seals and performed a confidence test on February 12. Subsequently, a second wet dress rehearsal was conducted on February 19, which was successful. ([space.com](https://www.space.com/space-exploration/artemis/nasa-will-fuel-up-its-artemis-2-moon-rocket-for-the-2nd-time-on-feb-19-will-it-leak-again?utm_source=openai))

Therefore, the specific cause of the early termination of the first rehearsal was a hydrogen fuel leak at the launch pad.

<final_answer>
(D)
</final_answer>

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. Writer and philanthropist Anna Murdoch-Mann has died at the age of 81. During her 31-year marriage to Rupert Murdoch, she authored several novels. What was the specific subject of her 1988 novel, 'Family Business'?
(A) A memoir about her 31-year marriage to Rupert Murdoch.
(B) A fictionalized account of the 1969 abduction of Muriel McKay.
(C) A historical account of her time as a reporter for the Sydney Daily Telegraph.
(D) A chronicle of the succession battles between her children Elisabeth, James, and Lachlan.
(E) A multi-generational media dynasty.
explanation: The article states: "She penned several novels during their marriage, including the 1988 Family Business about a multi-generational media dynasty."
model_output:
Anna Murdoch-Mann's 1988 novel, "Family Business," is a fictionalized account of the 1969 abduction of Muriel McKay. Muriel McKay was the wife of Alick McKay, Rupert Murdoch's deputy, and was kidnapped in Hertfordshire, England, in 1969. The kidnappers had originally intended to abduct Anna Murdoch-Mann herself, but due to confusion, they mistakenly targeted Muriel McKay instead. Tragically, Muriel McKay was killed by her captors. ([aol.com](https://www.aol.com/articles/anna-murdoch-mann-mother-news-030225120.html?utm_source=openai))

In "Family Business," Murdoch-Mann explores themes of family dynamics, media influence, and the complexities of personal relationships within the context of a media empire. The novel delves into the challenges and intricacies of managing a family-run business, drawing parallels to her own experiences and observations. ([kirkusreviews.com](https://www.kirkusreviews.com/book-reviews/a/anna-murdoch-2/family-business-2/?utm_source=openai))

Therefore, the correct answer is (B) A fictionalized account of the 1969 abduction of Muriel McKay.

<final_answer>
(B)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. Actor Eric Dane has passed away 10 months after revealing his ALS diagnosis. Before his death, how did Dane specifically use his acting career to raise awareness about his condition?
(A) He appeared on an episode of 'Brilliant Minds' playing a firefighter struggling to accept an ALS diagnosis.
(B) He returned to the set of 'Euphoria' to film a final season centered around a character with motor-neurone disease.
(C) He starred as an on-screen surgeon with ALS in the medical drama 'Grey's Anatomy'.
(D) He produced and starred in a documentary video for Target ALS to raise $1bn in federal funding.
(E) He played a clinician in an Oxford University-sponsored film about the rapid progression of neurodegenerative brain disorders.
explanation: The text states: "He also used his craft to raise awareness, appearing on an episode of medical drama Brilliant Minds in November as a firefighter struggling to accept help after an ALS diagnosis."
model_output:
Eric Dane, known for his roles in "Grey's Anatomy" and "Euphoria," publicly revealed his diagnosis of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) in April 2025. Following his diagnosis, he became an advocate for ALS awareness and research. In November 2025, he guest-starred in an episode of NBC's "Brilliant Minds," portraying a firefighter with ALS. Dane expressed that this role was both challenging and cathartic, as it mirrored his personal experiences with the disease. ([yahoo.com](https://www.yahoo.com/entertainment/tv/articles/why-eric-dane-only-play-082732768.html?utm_source=openai)) He also joined the board of directors of Target ALS, a nonprofit organization dedicated to ALS research, aiming to raise awareness and funds for the disease. ([alsnewstoday.com](https://alsnewstoday.com/news/actor-eric-dane-joins-target-als-board-following-own-diagnosis/?utm_source=openai))

The other options do not align with known information about Dane's efforts to raise awareness about ALS.

<final_answer>
(A)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. A woman was recently charged after abandoning her two-year-old dog at Las Vegas's Harry Reid International Airport. When questioned by police at the departure gate, what specific reason did she give for leaving the animal behind?
(A) She claimed she had arranged for the charity Retriever Rescue of Las Vegas to collect the animal.
(B) She stated the airline wouldn't let her fly with the dog and claimed it had a tracking device, implying it would return to her.
(C) She stated that she had lost the required documents for the golden doodle to fly as a service dog.
(D) She claimed the dog was aggressive and had to be left tied to a baggage sizer for passenger safety.
(E) She said she was fleeing a hostile situation and could not afford the fees to board the animal.
explanation: According to the police statement in the article: "When asked why she abandoned the dog, she stated the airline would not allow her to fly with it and claimed the dog had a tracking device - implying it was acceptable to leave the animal behind and it would return to her".
model_output:
The woman was denied boarding at Harry Reid International Airport because she failed to complete the necessary online documentation to travel with her dog as a service animal. After being denied a boarding pass, she left the dog tied to a baggage sizer at the JetBlue ticket counter and proceeded to her departure gate. When questioned by police, she claimed that the airline would not allow her to fly with the dog and stated that the dog had a tracking device, implying it would return to her. ([yahoo.com](https://www.yahoo.com/news/articles/woman-arrested-abandoning-dog-las-211531174.html?utm_source=openai))

Therefore, the specific reason she gave for leaving the animal behind was that the airline wouldn't let her fly with the dog and that it had a tracking device, implying it would return to her.

<final_answer>
(B)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. The Supreme Court has struck down President Trump's use of the IEEPA for global tariffs. According to analysis of the ruling's political impact, why might some of Trump's conservative allies in Congress be "breathing somewhat easier" following this decision?
(A) Because they now have explicit authorization to draft a new tariff bill before the midterm elections.
(B) Because the ruling guarantees that tariff revenue will remain in the US Treasury instead of being refunded.
(C) Because Trump's tariffs and their costs to consumers have been unpopular, making Republican candidates vulnerable to Democratic attacks.
(D) Because the decision forces America's trading partners to make concessions ahead of the State of the Union Address.
(E) Because it allows them to push forward with efforts to end birthright citizenship and reshape the federal government.
explanation: The article explains: "In fact, some of Trump's conservative allies in Congress may be breathing somewhat easier with this decision. The president's tariffs - and the costs they have imposed on consumers - have been unpopular among many Americans. Republican candidates in battleground states and congressional districts would have been open to Democratic attacks for supporting Trump's policies."
model_output:
The Supreme Court's recent decision to strike down President Trump's use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) for imposing global tariffs has significant political implications. This ruling is particularly impactful for conservative allies in Congress who have been concerned about the political fallout from these tariffs.

The tariffs, introduced in 2025, were intended to address trade deficits and other national emergencies, such as drug trafficking. However, they have been unpopular among consumers due to increased costs, making Republican candidates vulnerable to Democratic attacks. The Supreme Court's ruling invalidates these tariffs, potentially alleviating the political pressure on conservative lawmakers.

Therefore, some of Trump's conservative allies in Congress might be "breathing somewhat easier" following this decision because the tariffs and their associated costs to consumers have been unpopular, making Republican candidates vulnerable to Democratic attacks.

<final_answer>
(C)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. The Supreme Court's decision to invalidate some of President Trump's emergency tariffs has shifted the global trade landscape. Despite the highly tariffed environment last year, what specific factor allowed China's trade to still flourish with the US?
(A) A massive pivot toward agricultural exports to the European Union and the UK.
(B) The collection of $240bn in tariff revenues directly from US-based manufacturers.
(C) American importers absorbing the costs of clothing and toys without raising prices.
(D) A surge in imports of IT hardware into the US amid the AI boom.
(E) Successful negotiations that exempted Chinese goods from the "Liberation Day" tariffs.
explanation: The article explicitly states: "But China has still flourished, thanks to a surge in imports of IT hardware into the US amid the AI boom."
model_output:
Despite the highly tariffed environment last year, China's trade with the US continued to flourish primarily because American importers absorbed the costs of clothing and toys without raising prices. This strategy allowed Chinese exporters to maintain their market share in the US despite the tariffs imposed.

<final_answer>
(C)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. Following the Supreme Court's ruling against the IEEPA tariffs, President Trump has enacted new global tariffs using Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974. Under these new rules, what is the specific trade status of textiles and apparel imported from Costa Rica and Honduras?
(A) They are subject to a 10% tariff because the de minimis exemption was ended last year.
(B) They are subject to a 15% tariff as part of the new global temporary tariff structure.
(C) They are exempt from all tariffs solely because of the USMCA agreement.
(D) They face reciprocal industry-specific tariffs under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act.
(E) They remain duty-free under the Dominican Republic-Central America Free Trade Agreement.
explanation: The article notes: "Similarly, textiles and apparel from Costa Rica, the Dominican Republic, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, and Nicaragua will remain duty-free under the Dominican Republic-Central America Free Trade Agreement."
model_output:
Under the new global tariffs imposed by President Trump using Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, textiles and apparel imported from Costa Rica and Honduras are exempt from the 15% surcharge. This exemption is due to their duty-free status under the Dominican Republic-Central America Free Trade Agreement (CAFTA-DR). The CAFTA-DR allows for duty-free entry of qualifying textile and apparel products from member countries, including Costa Rica and Honduras, provided they meet the agreement's rules of origin. ([trade.gov](https://www.trade.gov/summary-cafta-fta-textiles?utm_source=openai))

Therefore, the correct answer is:

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>

(A) He was ordered to step down by a panel of judges who ruled his appointment unlawful.

(C) He was instructed by Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche to dismiss the grand jury indictments.

This decision led to his resignation, as President Trump expressed dissatisfaction with Siebert's refusal to pursue the case. Today is February 21, 2026. President Trump has stated he is considering a limited military strike on Iran to pressure its leaders over their nuclear program. According to analysis, what specific domestic political risk does a protracted, multi-week conflict with Iran pose for the Trump administration?
(A) It would lead to the immediate impeachment of the president due to narrow Republican majorities in Congress.
(B) It could provoke a direct military retaliation against the USS Gerald R Ford and USS Abraham Lincoln.
(C) It would derail ongoing negotiations with US Special Envoy Steve Witkoff in Geneva.
(D) It threatens to unite the liberal members of the Supreme Court against his executive powers.
(E) It threatens alienating parts of Trump's Maga base who supported his promise to end messy foreign entanglements.
explanation: The article states: "...a protracted, multi-week conflict with Iran threatens alienating parts of Trump's Maga base which were drawn to his campaign promise of an end to messy foreign entanglements."
model_output:
President Trump's consideration of a limited military strike on Iran to pressure its leaders over their nuclear program presents several domestic political risks. A protracted, multi-week conflict could alienate parts of Trump's MAGA base who supported his promise to end messy foreign entanglements. This base has consistently favored a more isolationist approach, emphasizing domestic issues over international military engagements. Engaging in a prolonged conflict in the Middle East could be perceived as a betrayal of these campaign promises, potentially leading to decreased support among these constituents.

Therefore, the specific domestic political risk is:

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>

(A) Because more than 1,000 businesses have already filed class-action lawsuits to reclaim them.
(B) Because the Supreme Court did not provide any instructions on refunds when striking down the tariffs.
(C) Because the US international trade court ruled that small businesses must be refunded before large corporations.
(D) Because combining Section 122 tariffs with Section 232 and 301 tariffs will result in unchanged tariff revenue.
(E) Because Democratic Governor JB Pritzker successfully obtained a court order for $1,700 refund cheques per household.
explanation: The article notes: "Speaking at an event in Dallas, Bessent said revenues already brought in through the IEEPA tariffs were 'in dispute' since the Supreme Court did not provide any instructions on refunds."
model_output:
The Supreme Court's recent decision to invalidate President Trump's tariffs imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) has led to significant financial and legal uncertainties. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has indicated that the approximately $130 billion in tariff revenues collected are considered "in dispute" primarily because the Supreme Court did not provide specific instructions regarding refunds when it struck down the tariffs. This lack of guidance has left the administration without clear directives on how to handle the funds, leading to potential legal challenges and the need for further judicial clarification. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/ee971f36fbd3a3876909c37188dfee57?utm_source=openai))

<final_answer>
(B)
</final_answer>

(A) That Lachlan Murdoch would eventually take full control of the news group.
(B) That the empire would face financial ruin due to one of the most expensive separations in history.
(C) That there would be a lot of heartbreak and hardship over the succession.
(D) That Wendi Deng would exert undue influence over the company's board of directors.
(E) That her children would permanently sever ties with the family business to avoid pressure.
explanation: The article states: "In an exclusive interview after her separation, Murdoch-Mann told Australian Women's Weekly, 'I think there's going to be a lot of heartbreak and hardship' over the succession for Murdoch's media empire."
model_output:

There's been such a lot of pressure that they needn't have had at their age." ([theguardian.com](https://www.theguardian.com/media/2001/jul/26/uknews?utm_source=openai))

This statement indicates her belief that the succession process would lead to significant challenges and emotional difficulties for her children. Therefore, the correct answer is (C) That there would be a lot of heartbreak and hardship over the succession.

<final_answer>
(C)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. Former 'Grey's Anatomy' star Eric Dane has died from ALS, a disease for which there is currently no cure. According to Professor Kevin Talbot of the University of Oxford, what is the primary reason why a cure for ALS has not yet been found?
(A) The rapid progression of the disease makes it impossible to administer experimental clinical trial drugs.
(B) Federal funding for ALS research has been consistently blocked by government bureaucracy.
(C) Pharmaceutical companies prioritize treating more common diseases like Alzheimer's and cancer.
(D) The condition does not have a single unifying cause.
(E) Patients typically refuse to take part in the necessary clinical research studies.
explanation: The article details: "Medical treatments and technologies can improve the quality of life of those with ALS, but no cure has been found. Professor Kevin Talbot, who runs the University of Oxford's Nuffield Department of Clinic Neurosciences, told the BBC this is because it does not 'have a single unifying cause'."
model_output:
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a complex neurodegenerative disease characterized by the progressive degeneration of motor neurons, leading to muscle weakness and atrophy. The exact cause of ALS remains elusive, but it is generally accepted that the disease results from a combination of genetic susceptibility, age-related cellular changes, and environmental factors. ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24010870/?utm_source=openai))

Professor Kevin Talbot, a leading expert in ALS research, has emphasized that ALS is not a single disease with a unified cause. Instead, it is considered a clinicopathological syndrome resulting from a complex convergence of genetic susceptibility, age-related loss of cellular homeostasis, and possible environmental influences. ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24010870/?utm_source=openai))

Given this understanding, the primary reason a cure for ALS has not yet been found is:

(D) The condition does not have a single unifying cause.

<final_answer>
(D)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. In addition to broad global levies, President Trump has made other recent changes to US trade rules. Last year, the president ended a specific policy known as the "de minimis exemption". What exactly did this exemption allow?
(A) An exemption that allowed goods from Canada and Mexico to avoid the fentanyl tariffs under the USMCA.
(B) A policy that permitted critical minerals and pharmaceuticals to be imported without reciprocal tariffs.
(C) A rule that shielded small businesses from pursuing costly refunds for unlawful IEEPA taxes.
(D) A diplomatic agreement that kept tariff levels at an average of 6% for European imports.
(E) A rule that allowed goods valued at $800 or less to enter the US without paying any tariffs.
explanation: The article concludes by noting: "At the same time, Trump said he is keeping up tariffs on low-cost goods. Last year, he ended what was known as the de minimis exemption, which had allowed goods valued at $800 or less to enter the US without paying any tariffs."
model_output:
The "de minimis exemption" was a U.S. trade policy that allowed imported goods valued at $800 or less to enter the country without paying customs duties or taxes. This exemption facilitated the entry of low-value shipments without the need for full customs documentation. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/De_minimis?utm_source=openai))

In July 2025, President Trump signed an Executive Order suspending this exemption, effective August 29, 2025. This change required all imported goods, regardless of value, to be subject to applicable duties and taxes. ([whitehouse.gov](https://www.whitehouse.gov/fact-sheets/2025/07/fact-sheet-president-donald-j-trump-is-protecting-the-united-states-national-security-and-economy-by-suspending-the-de-minimis-exemption-for-commercial-shipments-globally/?utm_source=openai))

Given this information, the correct answer is:

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>
